data.title: Killam Apartment REIT: Double-Digit Lease Spreads Make It A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

As it has been more than two years since I last discussed Killam Apartment REIT (TSX:KMP.UN:CA) (OTC:KMMPF), the recently published Q2 results offer perhaps a good opportunity to have another look at this Canadian residential REIT, focusing on Atlantic Canada.

Robust Results

As you may know, the net income of a real estate investment trust isn't the best way to judge how attractive it is. The FFO and AFFO calculations are much more reliable, as they, of course, exclude the changes in the valuation of the real estate portfolio. As you can see below, the starting point is the net income as reported by Killam, and after making the necessary adjustments, we see the reported FFO is approximately C$39.4M. This works out to roughly $0.32 per share. That's stronger than the result in the first quarter of the year, as that first quarter tends to be the weakest for Killam.

What I mainly like about the Canadian real estate trusts is the fact that the maintenance CapEx is included in the AFFO calculation, unlike their US-based and European counterparts. As you can see below, the REIT continues to use approximately 5.6 million Canadian dollars per quarter as its anticipated maintenance capex. This results in a total AFFO of just over C$33.6M, which works out to C$0.27 per share. The second quarter once again was stronger than the first quarter, and the total AFFO per unit came in at approximately C$0.50 in the first semester.

The seasonality we see is mainly related to the manufactured housing property portfolio owned by Killam, as there are 9 seasonal resorts that generate 60% of their net operating income (‘NOI’) in the third and (and somewhat in the) fourth quarter of the year. This means the third quarter (with some spillover effects into the fourth quarter) usually is the best quarter for Killam, and investors are warned not to just simply extrapolate the H1 results in an attempt to calculate the full-year AFFO per share.

It's also interesting to see the management team estimate the lease spread to be approximately 13% right now, as the market rents are still consistently higher than the current contractual rents. The lease spread has obviously come down in the past few years as Killam has been able to renew leases at market rents versus the rent in place.

The image above also shows there's a big difference in lease spreads between the different cities where the company is active at.

With a current annualized rental revenue of approximately C$286M, a 13% uplift would indicate an additional C$37M in rental income without really incurring any additional expenses. This could have a potential positive impact of approximately C$0.30 per share (including the dilutive impact of the exchangeable units), and this will slowly be realized when new rental agreements are signed.

The Distribution Remains Very Well Covered

Killam Apartment REIT currently pays a monthly distribution of C$0.06, which works out to C$0.72 per share per year. Based on the current share price of C$17.92 (Thursday’s closing price), this results in a dividend yield of just over 4%.

And considering we can expect a full-year AFFO per share of approximately C$1.10, this represents an anticipated payout ratio of just 65%. This means the company retains 35% of its AFFO, and this results in an annualized cash retention of approximately C$45M which could be used for new projects or to reduce the net debt (with a debt-to-total assets ratio of less than 40%, Killam doesn’t really have to reduce its net debt, so it can focus on potential growth opportunities).

In any case, as I expect the AFFO/share to increase, I also expect distributions to increase at a similar pace; a low-single-digit annual increase seems pretty likely going forward.

Investment Thesis

Although the share price remained pretty strong, I think Killam offers the best of both worlds; the 4% distribution is attractive and very well covered, and I anticipate the payout ratio to decrease as the lease spreads indicate the rental income will continue to increase. Even if I would assume the average cost of debt would increase to 4%, this would have a negative impact of approximately C$0.05/share. So, based on the current leasing spreads and anticipating all other elements remaining unchanged, I see a clear path towards C$1.3-1.35 per share in AFFO, although it will take some time to realize this.

Based on the current share price and AFFO anticipations, Killam doesn’t appear to be very cheap at 16x AFFO. But I see quite a bit of upside potential thanks to the leasing spreads, and this should also boost the distribution down the road.

I have a small long position in Killam as I didn’t get a full fill during the April weakness, but after seeing the robust H1 results, it’s now near the top of my list to add to my position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Back To Operations
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum (OXY) had been making what management stated were cost-lowering acquisitions for some time now. But now it appears that management will concentrate on those acquisitions to increase the profitability of the company. Now whether that means management is "off the market" for still more acquisitions or whether there are no bargains out there is anyone's guess. It could be a strategy change as well. But that likely means that the future will feature debt repayments first, followed by the resumption of the preferred stock redemption. All of that is probably good news for shareholders.

The oil and gas industry has been in a steady downtrend ever since the spike in prices back in 2022. But it likely has been more than that, as both price-earnings ratios are historically low and the ratios of enterprise values to cash flow have likewise been low as well. Cyclical industries typically will return to favor. But waiting for that event to happen does require patience because Mr. Market is both volatile and has low future visibility. Nonetheless, the future fundamental improvements that would appear to mark this management earnings report would seem to point to future outperformance from the current price.

Debt Reduction

Even though Occidental Petroleum is investment grade, Mr. Market often focuses on debt levels. Since that is the case, a perceived debt level that is too high often holds back stock price appreciation until debt levels meet the approval of the market.

The last article covered debt reduction progress (among other things). Shown above is a continuing update on further debt reduction. Warren Buffett, who has purchased a significant stake in the company, likely covered the debt topic and has considerable faith in the future of the company. This was likewise covered as part of the debt reduction topic in the previous article.

But Mr. Market will wait for proof that the debt levels are where they should be. There are too many promises publicly made that do not happen as originally planned for all kinds of reasons. But debt is something that needs to be both properly serviced and paid down "no matter what happens". Therefore, the market waits for that accomplishment "no matter what happens" because it usually takes above-average management to get the job done on time in a volatile industry like this one.

Earnings

The market has long focused on earnings. But companies that make acquisitions often place as much of the cost as possible on assets that depreciate while also giving those assets a very conservative lifespan. That means that the depreciation component often soaks up considerable profitability while producing cash flow until the acquisition ages enough to not be quite so significant to income. Therefore, it is not all that unusual that this company is making likely low-cost accretive acquisitions. But the earnings may not show it right away.

Vicki Hollub, CEO, was originally criticized (a lot) for making the Anadarko acquisition. But in fiscal year 2022, the spike in prices allowed investors to see that she was absolutely correct in making that decision when Occidental posted record earnings per share. She probably had been correct long before that, but investors could not tell.

Lowering the company cost structure is often not an easy thing for investors to see unless management has a clearcut way to communicate it in the corporate presentations to investors. It often takes record earnings for the market to tell that management was indeed correct.

The same likely goes for the latest round of acquisitions. This management probably knows what it is doing and has made good moves. But the stock price is anything but accommodating because, in the eyes of the market, there is no proof.

But there is a lack of emphasis on the cash flow statement under various commodity conditions. Cash flow per share is likely the first place to show improvement after an acquisition. But many managements do not show or demonstrate improvement when it comes to perfectly good acquisitions.

The result is considerable doubt that gets reflected in the stock price.

Cash Flow Statement

What should happen here is that as noncore assets are sold and replaced with lower cost production, the results of that should show on the cash flow statement trend.

As shown above, the cash flow trend was actually upwards until commodity prices began to weaken. That may give a hint of the profitability improvements to come.

The combination of selling noncore (hopefully higher cost) assets, combined with the continued development of acquired lower cost production should eventually show as increased cash flow at various pricing points first. Earnings will follow as lower depreciation from more profitable wells lowers the corporate average depreciation over time. It may also affect lease operating expenses materially, depending upon the situation.

Summary

Occidental Petroleum has likely made a series of accretive acquisitions that have not been given time to show the benefits. As a result, the stock price is at a relatively low level. But that likely makes the stock a continuing strong buy at current levels with the idea that management knew what it was doing, and the results will show in future earnings reports as a result.

Occidental Petroleum is likely slowly moving towards a growth and income idea as a result. Large companies often move more slowly in any given direction than smaller companies. The volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices make that movement even harder to detect. Nonetheless, I suspect long-term investors like Warren Buffett have figured out that the benefits are worth waiting for even though he largely purchased stock at higher prices. He is not perfect by any means. But he has a damn good long-term record worth taking into account.

Risks

Any acquisition can disappoint. It would be rare for all of those acquisitions to not meet management expectations. Operations seem to be a strong point of this management. Therefore, it seems likely the acquisitions will benefit shareholders in the long run. But that is far from guaranteed.

The current political uncertainty puts a company like this "up for target practice" until all that uncertainty is replaced with a permanent and constructive agreement. This company makes money for the United States both in the country and worldwide. Therefore, one would expect that the current situation would be allowed to continue. But logic and politics are not the same thing.

data.title: D-Wave Q2: Quantum = Logic Defied
data.description:
D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:QBTS) stock is up over 2,000% in the past year, and it’s still losing money.

For Q2’ 25, D-Wave reported $3.1 million in revenue, while its stock trades with a market cap nearing $6 billion.

Let’s face it. The quantum computing narrative is very enticing. Investors are hoping this could be the next big thing, and they may be right. Even Wall Street is screaming “Strong Buy.”

But here’s what they’re ignoring, or choosing to ignore: D-Wave burns about $8 for every $1 it makes, it continues to raise capital by diluting shareholders, and its only real path to profit is by selling $40 million supercomputers that only a handful of buyers are interested in.

In this article, I’ll explain why I’m not buying the hype yet, and what I’m watching in Q3 that could change my mind, and why sometimes the best move… is to sit tight.

Spoiler: As a long term and conservative investor, I’m rating D-Wave as a Hold, for now.

D-Wave Q2 ‘25 Earnings

Looking at D-Wave’s Q2 ‘25 core metrics, revenue reached nearly $3.1 million, which is up around 42% year-over-year, surpassing market expectations by 21.79%.

But before we dismiss D-Wave’s Q2 revenue as embryonic, it’s worth noting that on a full-year basis, the numbers will likely improve. Notably, the majority of Q1’s $15 million in revenue came from its first-ever sale of their Advantage™ system to a research institution, the Jülich Supercomputing Centre. Taking this one-time purchase out of the picture would bring D-Wave’s Q1 ‘25 revenue to below $3 million.

In Q2, D-Wave’s gross profit also increased 42% year over year to $2 million, with a gross margin of 63.8% - relatively stable compared to the 63.6% gross of the same quarter last year. Since D-Wave maintains its pricing power on its quantum systems, it can contribute toward covering overhead expenses.

Maintaining a gross margin above 60% is not easy for a hardware company, and considering D-Wave isn’t even profitable yet, this is impressive.

Meanwhile, operating expenses rose 41% to $28.5 million, mainly driven by hiring more staff and higher R&D spending. It’s a positive indicator that D-Wave is pushing to scale its operations to potentially capture market share in quantum computing before competitors gain traction. But of course, this is unsustainable without notable revenue acceleration in the foreseeable future.

Operating losses have increased 41% to $26.5 million over the same period, which is concerning for D-Wave’s cash flow generation. This means the company is burning more money through its operations compared to its revenue growth.

D-Wave’s net loss, however, drastically increased to $167.3 million, or -$0.55 loss per share - mostly a result of $142 million in non-cash accounting charges related to warrant revaluations triggered by D-Wave’s rising share price: the stock appreciated more than 2,000% in just a year! So if you take these paper losses out, the net loss would only be around $25 million.

Although it's widely accepted that early-stage companies should invest heavily in R&D to remain competitive, this isn’t sustainable forever - partly the reason for my Hold rating.

On the other hand, D-Wave does have several developments in the pipeline, so what I’ll look for in Q3 will be crucial in determining whether I should upgrade my rating or not.

D-Wave Current Stock Profile

At the time of this writing, D-Wave’s stock sits at $17.17 per share, and is set to open a touch higher. That said, QBTS stock was trading around a dollar just over a year ago. This kind of growth rate either makes inventors rich or leaves them feeling excruciating pain for not buying more shares, or worse, not buying at all. The stock reached a $20.56 resistance level before stabilizing to where it is today, $17.

Where’s The Value?

Let’s be brutally honest. D-Wave’s current share price has almost nothing to do with its actual financial performance - at least as of now.

The company brought in just around $3.1 million in this quarter, yet it’s valued at nearly $6 billion. It’s like investors are paying around $222 for every dollar of D-Wave’s next year’s expected sales.

As far as I can tell, the quantum computing narrative has created a speculative craze that’s completely disconnected from financial fundamentals. I mean, even if D-Wave were to hit analysts’ aggressive revenue projection of $88.20 million by 2027, the stock would still be trading at around 68x forward sales - something that’s typically “accepted” for the most dominant and profitable tech players.

Bottom line? D-Wave is a startup that trades like a mega-cap.

Dilution Concerns

D-Wave also has a notable history of aggressive share dilution, which makes it particularly interesting. The company went public in 2022, and since then, it has increased its outstanding shares by 167%. Every time the company needs cash, which is quite frequently, existing shareholders get diluted, and actually, the most recent $175 million equity offering is an example of this pattern.

I mean, quantum computing might be the future, but if you pay today’s prices, it means we need everything to go perfectly just to break even, or worse, a notable decline in core metrics could trigger a life-questioning price correction.

What I’m Looking For In Q3

The upcoming Q3 report could help justify even a fraction of D-Wave’s current valuation.

Wall Street expects revenue for the quarter to come in north of $3.08 million, which translates to a 64.71% increase from the same period last year - it sounds exciting, until I remember the fact that it's still only $3 million (not with a “B”) in quarterly revenue for a company with around $6 billion market cap.

In terms of the bottom line, adjusted loss per share is expected at -$0.05, which would mean a 44.94% improvement from last year’s loss - no actual profits yet, but hey, at least losses are narrowing, hopefully.

For Q3, I’m going to be looking for improvements to the underlying business - figures that actually matter. I’ll ignore the hype and watch the Advantage2 system sales closely.

These quantum computing systems can be sold for up to $40 million, which is the “ballpark” for similar systems.

Anyone need a Quantum supercomputer? I know someone!

Jokes aside, I see Advantage2 as D-Wave’s only catalyst for real revenue growth.

I’ll also be on the watch for announcements that could indicate whether there’s a real market for D-Wave’s premium hardware.

I’ll also keep an eye on the Leap cloud platform’s customer metrics. E.g., are the pilot customers converting to higher-value contracts?

This is important to see whether the business is actually moving beyond experimental quantum projects to real production deployments. And, of course, I’ll be watching the burn rate or any mentions of future funding needs.

Even the smallest disappointments could trigger selloffs, and conversely, any genuine progress might justify the current market optimism.

Wall Street’s Take On D-Wave

Wall Street appears to be just as caught up in the quantum hype as retail investors. Out of 9 analysts who covered D-Wave in the last 3 months, 7 of them actually rated QTBS stock as “Strong Buy” while the other two rated it with regular “Buy.” That’s a pretty bullish take, which could make contrarian investors think for a second.

Price targets are also quite bullish. QTBS stock now trades at around $17, but on average, Wall Street expects the D-Wave shares to advance to $22.44 over the next year.

My Verdict

To all the D-Wave investors out there, I ask: How is a company that burns ~$8 for every dollar it brings in, has a market cap approaching $6 billion, and a stock trading at 200+ times sales, considered undervalued?

In all my years of trading, I’ve noticed that when everyone agrees something is a great investment, it often means all the good news is already baked into the price.

It reminds me of an old saying, "If your taxi driver suggests buying a stock, that's your cue to run."

And the fact that Wall Street has such a rosy outlook despite the company’s brutal financials means they’re betting more on the potential and much less on the business foundations.

When the hype around the quantum computing narrative cools off, I’m expecting to see analysts scrambling.

Moreover, Quant Ratings and Seeking Alpha analysts' ratings are already “Hold,” which I think is far more fitting for the company’s current financial and market position. Interesting, as I'm often in disagreement with Quant and Seeking Alpha analysts!

D-Wave’s Q2 ‘25 was unattractive to me, but that’s probably because of the kind of investor I am. Sure, there’s some potential growth drivers within its pipelines, though most of them are yet to materialize, or there’s no specific timeline that investors can prognosticate.

Although quantum computing is undoubtedly something that enterprises will likely utilize in the future, especially with how advanced AI has become, I’d prefer to wait for D-Wave to become self-sufficient first. For now, I don’t have the appetite for the risk it entails.

Until then, my Hold rating for D-Wave will remain unchanged.

data.title: QuantumScape: Undervalued Trailblazer
data.description:
Introduction

QuantumScape (NYSE:QS) is a company that is currently valued at around $5 billion while the company's revenue is still zero. This might be the reason why there is a 13% short interest, but I think that the market cap of QS is based on its first-mover advantage in a potentially explosive solid-state battery industry. Since solid-state battery technology has potential to disrupt the large market of EV batteries, QuantumScape's current $5 billion market cap does not look bad at all. The level of uncertainty surrounding the timing of full technology commercialization remains very high, but allocating a small portion of my portfolio to QS appears to be a promising opportunity to invest in a first mover at a relatively early stage of its business cycle. This rationale supports a 'Buy' rating.

Fundamental analysis

According to the latest 10-Q, QuantumScape is focused on the development and commercialization of its solid-state lithium-metal batteries. There is only one segment, and QS is still a pre-revenue company because it is not yet commercialized as of today.

However, QS has a first-mover advantage in a potentially explosive industry. The company is one of the solid-state battery technology pioneers since it has been developing it in partnership with Volkswagen Group over the last several years. Strong potential of the solid-state battery technology is backed by the secular shift of the global automotive industry from internal combustion engines (ICE) to electric vehicles (EV) as part of the broader shift to cleaner energy.

The pace of penetration of EVs across the world is impressive, but it could have been even faster if some of disadvantages of EVs compared to ICE vehicles are mitigated. According to a quite interesting survey by McKinsey, longer driving ranges, lower purchase prices compared with ICE, and higher charging speed are among the top-5 factors that would tip respondents in favor of an EV. As scientist Scott Gorman suggests, solid-state battery technology has solid potential to improve attractiveness of EVs as they provide several crucial advantages compared to the currently deployed battery technology.

Therefore, the solid-state battery technology has solid potential to help in accelerating the global shift to EVs, which will be beneficial for the entire industry. As a company that is involved in the development of this technology over the last decade, QS has a solid first-mover advantage. Moreover, it partners with the world's second largest auto manufacturer, Volkswagen (its PowerCo battery division). This partnership appears to be quite strong because recently parties agreed on revised terms which mean expansion of collaboration and increased budget for the next two years.

I think QuantumScape's management were able to win and maintain such a vital strategic collaboration with Volkswagen because they have been consistent in delivering on their promises. Additionally, they have a clearly outlined roadmap with strategic goals which will approach the company closer to commercialization.

In summary, QS holds a clear first-mover advantage in a potentially transformative industry that could disrupt the entire automotive sector. The company has outlined a step-by-step strategic roadmap and benefits from a strong partnership with Volkswagen. As a result, the fundamentals appear bullish.

Valuation analysis

QuantumScape's current valuation of around $5 billion is significantly lower than this year's peak of $8.3 billion. Saying whether it is good or bad for a company with zero revenue is difficult but let me try to explain why I believe that current valuation of QS looks quite good.

My bullish thesis is built around the idea that QS has a solid first mover advantage in an industry that has potential to disrupt the entire EV battery market. Therefore, we can approximately estimate the revenue generation capacity of QS based on growth projections for the EV battery market.

Analysts at Precedence Research project that the EV battery market will reach $740 billion by 2034, representing a massive potential TAM (total addressable market) for QS. However, a timeline stretching nearly ten years from 2025 is both long and uncertain. Therefore, it makes sense to focus on forecasts for the nearer term. For instance, the market is expected to exceed $200 billion by 2029, just four years from now, making this projection somewhat more reliable.

Wall Street analysts expect QS to generate approximately $1.1 billion in FY2029 sales. Dividing $1.1 billion by the $219 billion forecasted market size equals an excessively conservative 0.5% estimated market share for QS. This appears unfairly pessimistic for a company which is positioned at the forefront of the solid-state battery technology development and is moving towards commercialization quite successfully.

I acknowledge that four years from now is also quite remote, but my confidence in QuanumScape's ability to achieve this stage is reinforced by the recent Q2 earnings release. First of all, there is the aforementioned $130 million partnership expansion with Volkswagen's PowerCo, which is not just a 'letter of intent' but a real cash inflow for the next two years. The management also shared information that there is a joint development agreement with another major automotive OEM. This development signals growing traction and urgency among automakers to secure next-gen battery tech.

From a financial position perspective, the outlook also supports QuantumScape’s ability to remain successful through 2029. QS ended Q2 2025 with nearly $800 million in liquidity, and management has extended the company’s cash runway into 2029. This is six months better than previously guided. Therefore, my confidence in QuantumScape’s future success is grounded not only in its roadmap and technological achievements to date, but also in management’s prudent capital allocation and the company’s ample liquidity to continue financing R&D efforts.

Consequently, I believe Wall Street’s revenue growth forecasts significantly underestimate QS’s true potential. That said, as QS moves closer to commercialization, it is increasingly likely that we will see upward revisions to revenue growth projections. Therefore, I consider QS to be substantially undervalued.

Mitigating factors

As I said in the very first paragraph of my thesis, the level of uncertainty regarding full technology commercialization timing is extremely high. This makes QS an extremely risky investment with an uncertain future. We should keep in mind that potential aggressive long-term returns from disruptors like QS also mean that investors agree with extremely high risks.

QS is an extremely volatile stock, with its closing share price ranging from a low of $3.47 to a high of $14.64 over the past twelve months. Since there is still no revenue, investors mostly focus on news instead of the company's financial performance. As a result, the stock can decline by 10%–20% in a single day simply due to unfavorable headlines. The reverse is also true though. For this reason, I intend to remain invested in this stock over the next several years, without attempting to time the market. I caution readers that trying to time such an extremely volatile stock could lead to significant losses.

Conclusion

QS certainly deserves to be added to my portfolio with a modest allocation. Buying this potential disruptor with a first mover advantage while it is valued at $5 billion looks like a good entry point. The level of risk and uncertainty is extremely high, but I do not feel that I am overpaying for QS at $8.3 per share. Therefore, it is a 'Buy' for me.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Here's How To Digest Q2 Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Rocket Lab Corporation (NASDAQ:RKLB) Q2 '25 earnings are out, and it was a mixed bag, to say the least. For starters, the company posted a $144.4 million revenue figure, beating guidance. However, they also reported a bigger loss per share at -$0.13 against the -$0.10 consensus. Other than this, the quarter saw both their space systems and launch revenue grow by about $10 million each.

I feel a lot of investors only view Rocket Lab as a launch company, so I plan to go into some detail about the $97.9 million that makes up their space systems segment. Management also gave us a very bullish update on the Neutrons' progress. Something investors and I have been waiting on for quite some time. In brief, the company is still on track and plans to complete 3 Neutron rocket builds next year.

The stock itself swung rapidly during the call but eventually was almost unchanged at just a 2.5% drop. Since the run-up to the early $50 per share range a few weeks ago, the stock has come down to trade in the low $40s. Currently, at $44, it carries somewhat of a premium, but with so much to come from the company between now and 2027, I see more upside to the stock ahead. I give a rundown of earnings and explain why I remain bullish on Rocket Lab.

Q2 '25 in a Nutshell

Rocket Lab came through this time with a record-breaking Q2 '25; it’s a result that, if you've been following Rocket Lab for a while now, you should have expected. Total revenue came in at $144.5 million against the $135.4 million consensus, representing 36% year-over-year growth. It's a figure that represents just how strong momentum is across both its launch and space systems businesses.

Remember, Rocket Lab has a dual revenue engine: product revenue primarily from their spacecraft components, systems, and full satellite builds, and service revenue from launching the rockets we’ve all come to love watching go up. Revenue from space systems reached $97.9 million, up from $87 million last quarter. It’s a big number for them, most probably driven by ramping up production for large defense contracts like the Space Development Agency’s Tranche 2 Transport Layer.

Launch revenue, which of course includes Electron launch missions, came in at $46.6 million, growing a staggering 30% since Q1 '25. Rocket Lab executed five successful Electron launches in the quarter, all of which carry a hefty price tag, and benefited from increased international demand. Importantly, the company demonstrated significant gross margin expansion, increasing GAAP gross margins to roughly 32.1%, up 650 basis points from a year ago. On a non-GAAP basis, gross margins were even stronger at around 37%; they’ve achieved a higher-margin product mix this past quarter, particularly from custom spacecraft.

Looking forward to Q3 '25, the company actually raised guidance to a midpoint of $150 million. Wall Street is expecting somewhere in the $150.3 million range.

While Rocket Lab still remains unprofitable, the company is continuing to invest in long-term growth, particularly through heavy R&D spending and targeted M&A activity. The quarter saw research and development expenses rise to $66.1 million, up sharply from $39.9 million in the same quarter last year, a 65.7% increase. It’s a continued investment in the Neutron, which is now getting even closer with hardware tipped to be on route to the Launch Complex 3. R&D also includes design and manufacturing activity tied to a pipeline of defense-related satellite platforms, which I’ll get into in more detail later. Selling, general, and administrative expenses rose to $39.9 million, compared to $30.5 million a year ago, a figure driven in part by costs associated with the pending $275 million acquisition of Geost. Not cheap for Rocket Lab by any means, but it should help expand Rocket Lab’s presence in defense payloads and further round out its end-to-end mission capability.

Despite this bump up in spending, they’re still expanding their revenue base. They reported a record top line of $144.5 million and improved gross margins, which are beginning to absorb more of that operating cost structure.

In terms of profitability, the company reported a net loss of $66.4 million, or about $0.13 per share, vs the $0.10 loss Wall Street was betting on. This is compared to a $41.6 million loss a year ago. However, Rocket Lab remains well capitalized with $564 million in cash and equivalents, up sharply from $271 million at the end of 2024. The improved liquidity gives the company ample runway to fund development for the upcoming Neutron.

We saw revenue scales and margin expansion continue in a strong direction this earnings season. Rocket Lab is clearly progressing toward long-term profitability, which most analysts expect to see sometime in 2027.

Everything Other Than the Neutron

Now, when it comes to Rocket Lab, most will tell you they’re a launch company, go on about their Electron and soon-to-come Neutron rockets. However, those who follow Rocket Lab closely will know that the majority of their revenue does not, in fact, come from launches. About 68% of revenue this quarter came from their space systems segment.

So what is that exactly? From what we know, it's composed of three main revenue streams. Full satellite builds, of course, where they design and manufacture anything from spacebuses to satellite component subsystems. At the core of this segment are Rocket Lab’s customizable satellite buses, including the Photon, Pioneer, Explorer, and Lightning platforms. These buses can service a range of missions, from low Earth orbit constellations to pretty deep space exploration, as well as national security deployments.

For example, the Photon has been used in the past for NASA’s CAPSTONE mission to the Moon and is being prepared for interplanetary work, including an upcoming private mission to Venus. The Pioneer and Lightning buses are more focused on high-volume constellation deployments and defense missions. They can offer modular, high-power solutions. The Explorer bus, meanwhile, is tailored for cislunar and deep-space science missions, giving Rocket Lab clients full-stack delivery options for destinations far beyond LEO.

In addition to full spacecraft, Rocket Lab is actually a major supplier of space-grade components and subsystems, many of which are integrated back into its own buses. These include a whole host of things like reaction wheels, solar panels, star trackers, separation systems, command and data handling units, and power systems. Many of these products actually came from Rocket Lab’s acquisition of companies like Sinclair Interplanetary. These components have already flown on hundreds of spacecraft globally. This portion of the business is what I like seeing grow. It comprises a lot of recurring orders in an area where they hold strong margins. They’ve been doing this for a while and know where to save money.

Finally, Rocket Lab also offers something called mission and systems services, which include spacecraft systems engineering, mission integration, ground control, and on-orbit operations support. Yes, I know, these services are often bundled with full satellite builds, but I like to view them as stand-alone revenue segments since they're often for government agencies and large commercial operators. So essentially, Rocket Lab owns the full value chain, from spacecraft hardware to on-orbit servicing. In aggregate, the Space Systems segment represents the company’s fastest-growing and most diversified business line, and that's before we even get to the launches. So next time someone tells you they launch rockets, remember, that's not the whole story.

Finally, an Update on the Neutron

I thought management provided a detailed and pretty optimistic update on the Neutron during this earnings call. They remain on track for a potential first launch by the end of this year, which is great news for investors. The reason this launch is taking so long is that they're focusing on reliability and long-term scalability. They want the Neutron to eventually become as reliable and as in demand as the Electron. CEO Peter Beck described the project as entering an intense execution phase, with flight hardware for Neutron’s second stage already being shipped to Launch Complex 3. Launch Complex 3, if you’re wondering, is Rocket Lab's newly built pad at Wallops Island, Virginia, which is now substantially complete and scheduled for a ribbon-cutting event this month on August 28th.

Beck also noted that development work is happening in parallel across a couple different fronts, stating, “The schedule is not sequential… everything is happening in parallel,” and that while the company is pushing for a year-end debut mentioning, “we’re not going to rush and take stupid risks to get Neutron to launch before it's ready.” This is what I like to hear: if the Neutron was rushed and the launch failed in one way or another, the stock would trade down considerably.

Elsewhere, engine qualification is progressing rapidly, with the Archimedes engine now being hot-fired about 3 to 4 times per day as part of its final test campaign. The company also shared that its production line is now capable of producing one engine every 11 days, an impressive stat when I heard it. They expect to build three Neutron vehicles next year, which is massive news for investors. The Neutron will come with a much higher price tag than the Electron, so we can expect Neutron launches to cost clients at least $10 million and upward.

In terms of mission scope, Neutron is already included in the U.S. Space National Security Space Launch program and will actually be the first rocket to fly from Virginia under that contract. Beck views a successful Neutron as “getting to orbit and making sure the vehicle is ready to scale,” while partial success may still be acceptable for landing/reuse, which will, of course, iterate over time. I don't expect them to stick the landing on the first try. The company is clearly betting on Neutron to be a long-term national asset, designed to compete directly with Falcon 9 and meet the ever-increasing launch demands of government and commercial customers.

Valuation

Since the run-up this stock has had over the past few months, it carries a premium valuation. As per Seeking Alpha, they hold a forward price-to-sales ratio of 36.61 against a sector median of just 1.6. With a forward EV-to-sales ratio of 37.11 against a median of 2.08. That's a ridiculous 2,186% and 1,680% premium, respectively.

Now, it's worth noting here that the sector median is formed from a peer group in which only one other company launches or builds space systems. That being BWX Technologies (BWXT), a company that plays a role in building nuclear systems for space.

Without paying too much attention to the peer group, the company still carries a high valuation for a business that is yet to be profitable. The way I view this lofty valuation is that it only makes sense if it can continue to sustain this high revenue growth we’re seeing and eventually reach profitability by early 2027. They are already solidifying their place in space systems, particularly in reliable launches. But with ongoing losses and execution risk, the stock remains speculative to some, which I can understand. The stock will be very sensitive to delays going forward.

What's Next?

Next up on the calendar is the Virginia launch site ribbon-cutting event at the end of the month. Since the site is set for the Neutron launch, expect management to comment further on the progress at the event. For now, RKLB investors should remain bullish and look for entry points in the low $40 range.

data.title: CoreWeave: Core Needs A Collar
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) announced the acquisition of Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ) earlier this month. There are aspects of this deal I support. I support the $20.40 per CORZ share value that the buyer put on it based on the buyer’s market price. I support the ~66% premium to CORZ’s unaffected share price. I agree that the unaffected shares are worth ~$12.30 (I use $12 for the standalone downside and $20 for the upside in a deal and need no false precision; but numbers are reasonable approximations).

I own and like CORZ. It was a great opportunity coming out of bankruptcy and remains a good opportunity today. For background on their stock, I discussed it in Bitcoin And The Best Miner: Core Scientific.

CoreWeave was a frothy market IPO. All positive catalysts were priced in when the deal was announced. Last month, I discussed it in the self-explanatory Sell Your CoreWeave. It was up over 300% since IPOing, and should be back in the double digits per share price range once insider lockups expire next month. Since I started writing about investing, it is in a half dozen that least justified their price.

So, how can we solve a stock-for-stock merger between a buyer with an overvalued stock and a target with an undervalued stock? A collar. We know how much value the merger has to CoreWeave because they told us.

On top of CORZ’s $12 per share standalone value (its unaffected value according to the buyer), we CORZ owners should get our share of the deal’s value. I’d love all of it. I’d settle for half.

This acquisition accelerates our strategy to deploy AI and HPC workloads at scale. Verticalizing the ownership of Core Scientific’s high-performance data center infrastructure enables CoreWeave to significantly enhance operating efficiency and de-risk our future expansion, solidifying our growth trajectory. Owning this foundational layer of our platform will enhance our performance and expertise as we continue helping customers unleash AI’s full potential.

- Michael Intrator, NASDAQ:CRWV CEO.

CRWV’s recent stock price performance is a problem, just not my problem (as a CORZ owner). The correct ratio to capture my non-greedy half of the deal value is 0.153 CRWV per CORZ share today with a collar that fixes the deal value at that level.

It is worth noting that Two Seas Capital, Core Scientific's largest active shareholder, plans to vote against the sale to CoreWeave as proposed. Two Seas founder Sina Toussi wrote to fellow NASDAQ:CORZ owners that,

Two Seas Capital LP ("Two Seas" or "we") is an alternative investment management firm that has been a committed investor in, and staunch advocate of, Core Scientific, Inc. ("Core Scientific" or the "Company") since 2022. We were early members of the ad hoc equity committee, supported the Company's emergence by joining in the rights offering in early 2024, participated in both convertible note issuances, and have grown our equity stake over the past 18 months. Today, we beneficially own 19,122,842 shares of Core Scientific's common stock, representing approximately 6.3% of the Company, making us the Company's largest active shareholder.

We invested in Core Scientific because we believe strongly in the Company's ability to create substantial value as a first-mover in building critical, high-performance computing ("HPC") infrastructure at scale. Artificial intelligence ("AI") represents one of the most significant technological developments of this century. As the need for AI training and inference has grown, the demand for energy and HPC infrastructure has increased dramatically. Core Scientific – with its significant scale, ready access to low-cost power, and data center talent – is uniquely positioned to meet this growing demand and has a clear runway to compound growth for many years.

We are therefore disappointed that the Board of Directors has chosen to sell the Company to CoreWeave, Inc. ("CoreWeave") at this inadequate valuation. The proposed all-stock, uncollared structure leaves Core Scientific shareholders exposed to the high volatility of CoreWeave's share price with no protections on the value they will receive at or following close.

We are not philosophically opposed to a merger of these two parties. We are also investors in CoreWeave, believe in its strategy, and respect its management team. Moreover, we have advocated for this combination as we clearly recognize the strategic merits.

However, from our perspective as a shareholder of Core Scientific, the proposed sale materially undervalues the Company and unnecessarily exposes its shareholders to substantial economic risk. In our view, the transaction decidedly and unfairly favors CoreWeave at the expense of Core Scientific shareholders. The fact that Core Scientific's stock price declined by 30% in the days following the transaction announcement strongly suggests to us that other investors agree.

We would be happy to continue owning Core Scientific as an independent company for years to come. With the demand for power and compute infrastructure rising at an unprecedented pace, and supply remaining scarce, we believe the Company's most promising days lie ahead. In our view, there is no compelling reason to sell Core Scientific at an underwhelming valuation with a deficient structure.

To be clear, we welcome all suitors, including CoreWeave, to bid on Core Scientific. The Board's job, however, is to ensure that any such deal happens at a price that reflects the strategic value of the Company's assets – including the value of any synergies, the existence of which CoreWeave has already acknowledged. In our view, the current transaction does not come close to meeting that standard.

Accordingly, unless the terms are revised to address what we believe to be both the structural shortcomings and inadequate consideration, we intend to vote against the transaction and plan to solicit you, our fellow Core Scientific shareholders, to do the same. We do not believe this transaction should receive shareholder approval.

We will further articulate our perspectives, and our valuation work, in the coming weeks and look forward to engaging with you then.

Caveat

Negotiating leverage comes down to alternatives. CORZ’s standalone alternative is better than the current deal, justifying voting it down. But the broad range of potential recuts comes down to whether alternative bidders emerge. $12 per CORZ share is unjustifiable and $20 is justifiable based on CRWV’s own deal rationale. Within that broad range, it will come down to both sides’ next best alternatives.

Conclusion

The current 0.1235 CRWV per CORZ is a bad deal. 0.153 CRWV per CORZ share is minimally acceptable.

TL; DR

Core Scientific, Inc. shareholders should vote down this deal. They/we risk little and could be well rewarded. We should be willing to listen to better deal proposals.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
